worth_it_explanation: Curated deals are how a publisher packages audiences and inventory into a product a buyer can transact against in TripleLift. A package mapped to the wrong inventory, or one whose audience segment has gone stale, sends a buyer a deal that no longer matches what they think they bought, which costs spend and trust. The check reviews packages the publisher already has in place, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal a publisher can correct or retire.
intro: Curation in TripleLift is the practice of bundling audiences and inventory into deals a buyer can target through a Deal ID, which carries the terms of the agreement into the bid stream. This subcategory covers whether those curated packages are set up correctly against the inventory they claim to represent, and whether they stay accurate over time rather than drifting as audiences and inventory change. These checks confirm that a buyer transacting on one of the publisher's deals receives the audience and inventory the deal describes.

audit_checks.title: Curated deal packages are not mapped to the inventory they describe
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package promises a buyer a specific audience on specific inventory, and the Deal ID carries that promise into the bid stream. When the package targets the wrong ad units, formats, or audience segment, the buyer transacts against inventory that does not match the deal, so spend lands where it was not meant to and the deal underdelivers against what the buyer agreed to.

educate.base:
A curated deal in TripleLift bundles an audience and a slice of inventory into a package that a buyer targets through a Deal ID. In the OpenRTB protocol that powers the bid stream, the Deal ID lives inside the private marketplace (PMP) object on the bid request, and it tells the buyer that the impression is available under the terms of that specific deal. The accuracy of the package depends on the mapping behind that Deal ID matching what the buyer was told they were buying.

Two mappings have to be right for a curated package to deliver what it describes. The first is the inventory mapping, which connects the deal to the ad units, sites, and formats the package is meant to cover. TripleLift is known for native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats, so a package sold as native inventory that maps to display units, or one sold as premium-site inventory that includes long-tail sites, sends the buyer impressions the deal did not describe. The second is the audience mapping, which attaches the audience segment to the package so the buyer reaches the people the deal names rather than undifferentiated traffic.

The audience side travels through the bid request as first-party data. In the Prebid header-bidding integration that TripleLift supports as a Prebid-compatible adapter, audience segments are passed in the OpenRTB first-party data fields using the IAB segment taxonomy, so the segment a publisher attaches to a package is the segment a buyer can target. A package whose audience field is empty, or whose segment points at a taxonomy the buyer cannot read, leaves the buyer with inventory but not the audience the deal promised.

The audit question is whether each active package maps to the inventory and audience it claims to. A few signs point to a mapping that does not match the package description:
  • A package named for one format or site set targets ad units or sites outside that description
  • A package sold as audience-curated has no audience segment attached, or one the buyer's platform cannot interpret
  • The buyer's delivery reporting shows impressions on inventory or audiences the deal was not meant to include
You may want to confirm that each active package's inventory and audience mapping matches the deal a buyer agreed to. One approach is to walk each package from its description through to the ad units, formats, and audience segment it actually targets, and reconcile any gap.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages often lean on editorial context, such as a section, a content category, or a sponsorship-adjacent placement that a buyer is paying a premium to reach. You may want to confirm that a package sold around a content theme maps to the ad units on that content specifically, rather than a broader run-of-site mapping that dilutes the premium the buyer agreed to.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a curated package may promise a buyer an audience close to a purchase, such as visitors to product or category pages. You may want to confirm that the audience segment behind the package reflects that commercial intent and maps to the commercial pages, rather than a blended site audience that includes lower-intent editorial traffic the deal did not describe.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed configuration documentation for setting up and mapping curated packages is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each active curated package against the inventory and audience it describes, confirming that the ad units, formats, and audience segment behind the Deal ID match what a buyer agreed to.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift curation console
steps: Open the curation or deals section of the TripleLift console and list the active curated packages with their Deal IDs.
For each package, open it and record the inventory it targets, including the ad units, sites, and formats (e.g., native, display, CTV).
Record the audience segment attached to each package and the taxonomy it uses, noting any package with no audience attached.
Compare each package's targeting against its description or the terms agreed with the buyer, and note any package where the inventory or audience does not match.

buyer_delivery_reporting.label: Buyer delivery reporting
steps: For each active Deal ID, ask the buyer or pull the delivery report showing the sites, formats, and audience the deal actually served against.
Compare the delivered inventory and audience to what the package description claims.
Note any deal where impressions landed on inventory, formats, or audiences the package was not meant to include.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active package, the inventory and audience it targets and whether both match the package description and the terms agreed with the buyer. A package whose ad units, formats, and audience segment match its description passes. A package whose inventory or audience mapping diverges from the description, or whose audience field is empty when the package was sold as audience-curated, is a fail recorded with the package and the mapping that does not match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the inventory and audience mapping for each active package and whether each matches the package description above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of the curated deal packages on {{site_name}} maps to inventory or an audience that does not match what the package describes. A curated package carries its terms to a buyer through a Deal ID, so when the inventory or audience behind that Deal ID diverges from the description, the buyer transacts against impressions the deal did not promise and the deal underdelivers against what they agreed to. {{voice_recommend}} walking each active package from its description through to the ad units, formats, and audience segment it actually targets, then correcting any mapping that does not match before the deal serves further. After the correction, reconcile the buyer's delivery reporting against the package description to confirm the deal now delivers what it claims.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are left active without maintenance and go stale
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package describes an audience and inventory at the moment it was built, but both shift over time as audience segments expire, sites are added or removed, and formats change. A package left active without review keeps transacting against a description that no longer holds, so a buyer pays for an audience or inventory that has drifted from what the deal named.

educate.base:
A curated package is accurate on the day it is built, and less so on every day after unless someone maintains it. The audience segment behind a package can decay as the data provider refreshes or expires it, so a segment that once held a defined audience can shrink toward empty while the Deal ID stays live. The inventory behind a package shifts too, as sites join or leave the publisher's supply and as formats such as native, branded content, and connected TV (CTV) are added or retired. A package that is never revisited keeps selling the description it was given, even after that description stops matching reality.

Stale packages cost in two directions. A buyer transacting on a deal whose audience segment has decayed pays for reach against an audience that has thinned, so the deal delivers less than the buyer agreed to. On the publisher side, dead or near-empty packages clutter the deal list a buyer browses, which makes the genuinely active products harder to find and erodes the buyer's confidence that a Deal ID still means what it says.

The Deal ID itself does not signal age. In the OpenRTB protocol the deal travels through the private marketplace object on each bid request with no indication of when the underlying package was last reviewed, so the only way to know a package is current is to check it on a schedule. The IAB Tech Lab's Deals API addresses part of this by standardizing how static deal terms move from a seller's system to a buyer's, which reduces the manual re-entry that lets deal records drift apart, but it does not replace a publisher reviewing whether a package's audience and inventory still hold.

The audit question is whether a maintenance routine keeps active packages current. A few signs point to packages going stale:
  • Active packages exist that have not been reviewed since they were created, with no record of a last-reviewed date
  • Deal delivery has fallen toward zero on packages still listed as active, which suggests the audience or inventory behind them has decayed
  • The deal list includes packages built for sites, formats, or campaigns that no longer exist
You may want to confirm that active packages are reviewed on a schedule and retired when their audience or inventory no longer holds. One approach is to set a review cadence, record a last-reviewed date per package, and retire any package whose audience segment has decayed or whose inventory no longer exists.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages are often built around a campaign, a season, or a content theme that has a natural end. You may want to retire packages tied to a finished campaign or an expired sponsorship so the active deal list reflects what a buyer can still transact against, rather than carrying spent products that no longer have inventory behind them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, audience segments built around purchase intent can decay quickly as shopping behavior shifts between seasons and product cycles. You may want to review the intent segments behind commercial packages on a tighter cadence than evergreen editorial packages since a stale purchase-intent audience misrepresents the deal more sharply than a slow-moving content audience.

investigate.intro: Review the active curated packages for evidence of regular maintenance, looking at when each was last reviewed and whether its delivery suggests the audience or inventory behind it has decayed.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift curation console
steps: Open the curation or deals section of the TripleLift console and list every active curated package with its Deal ID and creation date.
For each package, record any last-reviewed or last-modified indicator, noting packages with no review since creation.
Record the current size or status of the audience segment behind each package, noting any segment that has decayed toward empty.
Identify packages built for sites, formats, or campaigns that no longer exist and flag them as candidates for retirement.

deal_delivery_reporting.label: Deal delivery reporting
steps: Pull delivery by Deal ID across a recent period and record impressions and revenue per active package.
Compare each package's recent delivery against its earlier delivery to see whether it has fallen toward zero while still listed as active.
Note any active package with little or no recent delivery, which points to an audience or inventory that has decayed behind a live Deal ID.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active package, when it was last reviewed and whether its recent delivery and audience segment suggest it is still current. A set of active packages reviewed on a recorded cadence, with stale packages retired, passes. Active packages with no review since creation, a decayed audience segment, or delivery fallen toward zero while still listed are a fail recorded with the packages affected and the signs of decay.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the last-reviewed date and recent delivery for each active package above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} keeps curated packages active without a maintenance routine, so one or more have gone stale as their audience or inventory drifted from what the deal describes. A package is accurate on the day it is built, but audience segments decay and inventory shifts, and a Deal ID carries no signal of age, so a buyer can pay for an audience or inventory that has thinned since the package was created. {{voice_recommend}} setting a review cadence, recording a last-reviewed date for each active package, and retiring any package whose audience segment has decayed or whose inventory no longer exists. After the review, confirm that the active deal list reflects only packages a buyer can still transact against as described.
